title: Dimensionality-Driven Electronic and Orbital Transitions Mediating Interfacial Magnetism in LaNiO3/CaMnO3 Observed In Situ
authors: B-A. Courchene, A. Hampel, S. Beck, J. R. Paudel, J. D. Grassi, L. A. Lapinski, A. M. Derrico, M. Terilli, M. Kareev, C. Klewe, A. Gloskovskii, C. Schlueter, S. K. Chaluvadi, F. Mazzola, I. Vobornik, P. Orgiani, J. Chakhalian, A. J. Millis, A. X. Gray
abstract: Emergent magnetic states at oxide interfaces arise from the interplay of charge transfer, orbital reconstruction, and dimensional confinement, offering a route to engineered correlated-electron behavior in nanoscale spintronic materials. Here, we combine in situ synthesis, polarization-dependent angle-resolved photoelectron spectroscopy, X-ray magnetic circular dichroism, and first-principles electronic-structure calculations to investigate LaNiO3/CaMnO3 superlattices. We show that reducing the LaNiO3 thickness drives a metal-insulator transition accompanied by loss of electronic coherence and an orbital-polarization crossover in the ultrathin limit. These changes weaken charge transfer across the interface and suppress the interfacial Mn magnetic moment in CaMnO3, revealing that the emergent ferromagnetic state is directly governed by electronic confinement in LaNiO3. The insulating state and orbital reconstruction are reproduced by density functional theory combined with dynamical mean-field theory. Together, these results establish a direct and tunable coupling among electronic, orbital, and magnetic degrees of freedom in oxide heterostructures.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the magnetic properties of LaNiO3/CaMnO3 interfaces are controlled by the thickness of the LaNiO3 layer. As the layer becomes thinner, a metal-insulator transition and orbital reconstruction occur, which suppresses the interfacial magnetism. This reveals a tunable coupling between electronic, orbital, and magnetic degrees of freedom in oxide heterostructures.
main_problem: Investigating how dimensional confinement and thickness reduction in LaNiO3/CaMnO3 superlattices drive electronic, orbital, and magnetic transitions at the interface.
main_result: Reducing LaNiO3 thickness triggers a metal-insulator transition and orbital-polarization crossover, which in turn weakens charge transfer and suppresses the interfacial Mn magnetic moment.
method: The study combines in situ synthesis, polarization-dependent ARPES, X-ray magnetic circular dichroism (XMCD), and first-principles calculations using DFT combined with DMFT.
model_or_system: LaNiO3/CaMnO3 oxide superlattices, specifically focusing on the thickness-dependent behavior of the LaNiO3 layers.
key_observables: Electronic coherence, orbital polarization, charge transfer across the interface, and the interfacial Mn magnetic moment.
important_parameters: LaNiO3 layer thickness (dimensional confinement), electronic-structure parameters, and orbital-polarization crossover.
main_assumptions: The study assumes that the observed insulating state and orbital reconstruction can be accurately captured by DFT+DMFT calculations.
paper_structure: The paper presents an investigation of oxide heterostructures through in situ synthesis, followed by spectroscopic analysis (ARPES, XMCD) and theoretical validation via DFT+DMFT to establish the coupling between electronic, orbital, and magnetic degrees of freedom.
analysis_basis: abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
